with random best respone: [0.64269053 0.19060862 0.16670085]

without:  [0.71224517 0.05793004 0.22982479]


.4, .5, .1     (2)

.343, .6, .057 (3)

.296, .666, .037 (4)

changing every thousand: [0.90074304 0.00813857 0.09111839]

as player 2: [0.35553589 0.29868724 0.34577687]

umnep1: .48, .4, .12
umnep2: .32, .4, .28

^
|
\ scissors is doubled, when it's bigger, paper increases and scissors decreases. This is true of both players.




with:  [0.68662819 0.14587169 0.16750013]
without: [0.66105072 0.27209936 0.06684992]
changing every thousand, with: [0.32656696 0.54145512 0.13197791]


- changing every thousand does seem to make it start to veer toward a different equilibrium, as it doesn't when it changes every time. 

- i think the main thing making the resultant strategy non-equilibrium was that i had the best response only that of player one, so it worked against player two, but led to the unpredictible behavior against player one. fixing that helped both, although the best response did seem to help quite a lot, and without it it still plays way too little paper.

- I added lazy cfr to the chart, it runs quickly, but i'm not sure how to factor in training/model building time. I couldn't run it past connect four due to that, though i'm sure once it's built it will be well under a second.


-general idea to learn all equilibria simultaneously in a family of games, proof of concept showing it works. what do we want to show theoretically/empirically to build a case that this works? what theory explains when/why it works? 


ROCK

ideas for wcp:
    alphazero
    cfr variants, like deep monte-carlo and lazy
    https://arxiv.org/abs/2001.07118, https://causalincentives.com/ - causal incentives
    
    
    
i took out lazy cfr and added os-mc-cfr. As expected, based on the fact it is used with a low iteration count in my method, it is always faster, but fares far worse in large games. I included the training time, which seems to grow, though it doesn't get impractical for any of my examples.

i've plotted


i've made a chart for the calculated equilibria

ideas for wcp:
    alphazero   -   fun but a lot of work, atypical cs papers written by large team/long time, hard to follow.
    cfr variants, like deep monte-carlo and lazy
    https://arxiv.org/abs/2001.07118, https://causalincentives.com/ - causal incentives


i took out lazy cfr and added os-mc-cfr. As expected, based on the fact it is used with a low iteration count in my method, it is always faster, but fares far worse in large games. I included the training time, which seems to grow, though it doesn't get impractical for any of my examples.


i've plotted some of the resultant data on rcfr, i need to collect a lot more, because it looks pretty ugly now, but it does seem far better than the 

$ says "show that there's room to be closer to equilibria, show what can go wrong if you don't consider that. It could perhaps be instructive (you want to exploit a no regret guarantee). why does guaranteeing no regret tend to NE? Where does that argument break down in this context?" "How do ppl deal with the fact that RCFR doesn't actually minimize regret, as it is somewhat approximatory."

distance over time - exploitability/difference from equilibrium . plot this.
